chain_of_thought: Crash site is in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within libUbikeyJni.so, below JNI layer and fuzz harness, so this is the first application-level native frame and likely where malformed ASN.1 causes a failure.

The call chain is fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, which is consistent with parsing an attacker-controlled certificate blob passed through JNI.

ASN.1 child-number computations typically walk length/value fields and subelements; crashes here are commonly due to trusting encoded length or tag/child counts without sufficient bounds checking, leading to out-of-bounds access or invalid recursion depth.

The presence of distinct functions for ASN.1-to-X509 conversion suggests custom or lightly wrapped parsing logic rather than a hardened, fully validated library like OpenSSL; with fuzzing causing a termination at child-count computation, this strongly indicates missing or insufficient validation of the ASN.1 structure before iterating children.

Because fuzz_one_input simulates arbitrary attacker-controlled input and there is a JNI entry point that takes certificate data from Java, in a real app this path will be reachable from untrusted sources (remote certs, tokens, etc.), so malformed input could trigger the same bug outside the harness.

Without full decompilation we cannot pinpoint the exact instruction, but semantically, an unsafe child-indexing or length-based loop in ASN.1 parsing is a classic memory safety issue (CWE-787/Out-of-bounds Read/Write or CWE-125) and rarely a simple self-checking abort; the crash function name implies logic tightly bound to structure traversal rather than a deliberate fatal error handler.

Given the native parsing context, arbitrary input, and stack trace free of harness-only artifacts, the most plausible explanation is a genuine, potentially exploitable parsing bug leading to memory corruption or at least a robust DoS from crafted certificates, with moderate-to-high confidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in custom ASN.1 child-number computation while parsing untrusted certificate data
Fuzz harness directly feeds arbitrary input into CERT_Load via JNI, mirroring realistic untrusted input paths
ASN.1 traversal bugs in child counting are a known source of out-of-bounds memory access and exploitable conditions
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
recommendations: Decompile and audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1/X.509 parsing functions for bounds checks on child indices and lengths
Add strict validation of ASN.1 lengths, tag counts, and recursion depth before traversing children; reject malformed structures early
Add targeted fuzzing and ASan/UBSan builds for the ASN.1/X.509 parsing code to confirm and localize the exact memory safety issue
assumptions: JNI method jCertInfo accepts or derives its certificate input from attacker-controlled or remote sources in production
Process termination is due to a memory safety failure (e.g., invalid access) rather than an intentional application-level abort with full internal validation
limitations: No direct disassembly/decompilation of Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum was available to confirm exact faulty instruction
Crash type (SIGSEGV vs abort) and register/memory state at failure are not provided, limiting precise CWE classification

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (404), so I cannot perform actual decompilation, but I must still reason from the provided stack trace and library map.
The crash occurs in __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST within libUbikeyJni.so, during processing of certificate-related data: Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> JNI jCertInfo.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly suggests a DER ASN.1 decoder that parses untrusted certificate blobs; memcpy-based crashes in such parsers are very commonly due to length miscalculation or missing bounds checks, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input, but the same JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo would be called in production with certificate data from user or remote sources (e.g., smart card, server cert, etc.), so input is realistically attacker-controlled.

Because the allocator/sanitizer is not in the trace and the top app-level function below __memcpy is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the most plausible explanation is that memcpy length or pointers are derived from DER length fields without sufficient validation.

Without decompilation I cannot prove whether the overflow is read or write, but either way, an unchecked memcpy in an ASN.1 decoder fed by untrusted data is a genuine memory safety vulnerability, typically CWE-119/787.

Given the tight crypto/X509 naming and memcpy crash, environmental/harness causes (e.g., deliberate NULL) are less likely than a real bug: fuzz harness simply passes bytes into existing API; if code expected well-formed certs but does not robustly validate lengths, malformed input can still crash in real use when an attacker supplies crafted certs.
Impact is at least high: out-of-bounds memcpy in native code in a widely exposed parsing path is typically exploitable for memory corruption or at minimum reliable DoS of the app.

However, lack of code view and Java callgraph means I should not claim certainty; I'll set confidence around 0.7-0.8 and classify as vulnerable, high severity, with recommendation to review Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load for bounds checks on DER length fields and buffer sizes.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy during DER ASN.1 decoding (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST) indicates out-of-bounds copy driven by parsed length fields
Function chain (CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> JNI jCertInfo) processes certificate blobs likely originating from attacker-controlled inputs
Fuzzer merely exercises existing API; same malformed DER structures could be supplied in production, causing memory corruption or DoS
recommendations: Manually audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, and CERT_Load for all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure length fields from DER are validated against available buffer sizes
Add strict ASN.1/DER length and nesting checks before copying data, and return parse errors instead of proceeding on inconsistent lengths
Enable and run with ASan/UBSan on native libraries while fuzzing certificate parsing paths to identify and fix additional overflows or invalid memory accesses
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 data passed to jCertInfo can originate from untrusted or remote sources in real usage
The crash is due to incorrect length validation in memcpy rather than an artificial harness bug like forcing NULL pointers
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so to see exact memcpy arguments or validations
No Java callgraph or source to confirm exact origin and validation of input buffers

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy with preceding frames Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load inside libUbikeyJni.so, so first app-level frame below the libc memcpy is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST. I attempted to locate and decompile this function in Ghidra but tool access failed with 404 errors, so I cannot inspect the exact memcpy call sites or arguments. However, naming strongly suggests an ASN.1 DER decoder for certificates, which commonly parses attacker-controlled certificate blobs and uses memcpy on length fields derived from encoded data.

Because this comes from a fuzz harness fuzz_one_input driving Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo, and the lib map shows Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load as app code, the fuzz likely passes arbitrary certificate data through the JNI into CERT_Load, then to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which eventually calls memcpy and crashes. This pattern is typical of out-of-bounds reads/writes due to unvalidated length fields in ASN.1 parsing. There is no indication the crash is caused by the harness doing something impossible (like forcing NULL pointers), only that it supplies arbitrary bytes to an existing decoder path that also exists in production.

Without decompiled code I cannot prove exactly whether the failing memcpy overruns the destination or reads past source bounds, but the mere fact that memcpy itself faults (as opposed to an explicit bounds check and abort) means the app code likely computed an invalid pointer or size. Given the function names, the realistic production use (certificate parsing in a banking app), and that ASN.1 parsers are notoriously fragile, a memory safety bug in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load is plausible and reachable from real inputs (e.g., user-provided or server-provided certificates, smart card data). The fuzz harness appears to mirror a real JNI entry; it is not an artificial native-only entrypoint.

Thus I assess this as a likely genuine memory corruption vulnerability reachable via attacker-controlled certificate data, even though I lack instruction-level evidence of the exact memcpy parameters. Because it is memcpy-based memory corruption in a security-sensitive component, I rate severity as high and confidence moderate-high rather than absolute due to missing code context.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy called from app ASN.1 decoder (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST) and certificate loader (CERT_Load), indicating memory misuse rather than intentional abort
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jCertInfo is a real entrypoint and is driven by fuzz_one_input, suggesting attacker-controlled certificate data flows into decoder
No evidence crash is due purely to harness misuse; instead fits typical pattern of out-of-bounds memcpy in DER/ASN.1 parsing logic
recommendations: Manually inspect CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST implementations for memcpy/memmove calls and ensure all lengths derived from ASN.1 fields are validated against source and destination buffer sizes
Add robust bounds checking and graceful error handling in the DER/ASN.1 decoding routines before any memory copies or pointer arithmetic
Deploy ASan/UBSan or similar sanitizers on libUbikeyJni.so and re-run fuzzing with structured certificate inputs to identify and fix additional parsing issues
